texts.full_text: \pm}$ and photon selection requirements are the same as used for the ${J/\psi}{\rightarrow}\gamma\gamma\gamma\pi^+\pi^-$ final state, and the event selection is similar, except that here at least two photons are required in the event. The photons and charged tracks are kinematically fitted to ${J/\psi}{\rightarrow}\gamma\gamma\pi^+\pi^-$ assuming four-momentum conservation, and $\chi^2\leq20$ is required. When there are more than two photons, the kinematic fit is repeated using all possible photon combinations, and the one with the smallest $\chi^2$ is kept. The photon with the higher energy is considered to be the radiative photon from the ${J/\psi}$ decay. Figure 9 shows the invariant mass distribution of $\gamma\pi^+\pi^-$ for the candidate events where an ${\eta^{\prime}}$ signal is evident. Figure 9 shows the result of a fit to the $\gamma\pi^+\pi^-$ invariant mass distribution that follows a similar procedure as that for the fit to the $\gamma\gamma\pi^+\pi^-$ distribution of the previous section. The fit yields $23243\pm229$ ${\eta^{\prime}}$ signal events. The MC-determined detection efficiency for ${J/\psi}{\rightarrow}\gamma{\eta^{\prime}},{\eta^{\prime}}{\rightarrow}\gamma\pi^+\pi^-$ is $\varepsilon=(25.02\pm0.10)\%$. SYSTEMATIC ERRORS ================= Systematic errors in the branching fraction measurements mainly originate from photon identification (ID), MDC tracking efficiency, particle ID, kinematic fitting, mass resolution, $\pi^0$ reconstruction, and parameterizations of background shapes. Photon identification --------------------- The efficiency for photon ID is discussed in Ref. [@lism]. It is found that the relative efficiency difference between data and MC simulation for high energy photon detection is about 0.8% per photon, while for low energy photons, the difference is around 2% per photon. Since the energy of the radiative photon in ${J/\psi}{\rightarrow}\gamma\pi^0,\gamma\eta$, and $\gamma{\eta^{\prime}}$ is high, and the energies of the photons from pseudoscalar particle decays are low, the total systematic error due to photon ID is taken as $(0.8+2.0n)\%$, where $n$ is the number of photons from the pseudoscalar particle decay. One of the first nongov-ernmental relief organizations to bring supplies to the Haitian people, the Volunteer Ministers arrived before the airport even re-opened. In the initial aftermath, the Volunteer Ministers distributed tons of supplies and set up and operated the first and only medical and evacuation center. In the ensuing months, Volunteer Ministers were active in providing medical relief, medical and hygiene supplies, food, and in the long-term job of sanitation and disease prevention. The main band DNA is likely to represent the majority of the DNA molecule, which is the genome of the Chinese hamster. The two separate DNA components that hybridise with the labelled probe, on the other hand, are likely to represent the two separate and distinct genes that code for the 5S RNA molecule. The fact that one of the components is slightly heavier and one is considerably heavier than the main band DNA, suggests that the two genes are located on separate and distinct chromosomes, or are located on the same chromosome but are separated by a region of DNA that is denser than the surrounding DNA. The hybridisation of the labelled probe with the two separate DNA components suggests that the gene coding for 5S RNA is a separate and distinct gene that is located on a separate chromosome or is separated by a region of DNA that is denser than the surrounding DNA. texts.full_text: have hl-line+ installed and have tried reducing the hl-line-overlay-priority option but that does not seem to work. The entire line still get the same background color. Are there any other modes or settings I can set to make this work? A: highlight-symbol-mode uses text properties, not an overlay. hl-line-mode uses an overlay, not text properties. Overlays always take precedence over text properties, no matter how low the overlay priority is. So without some coding or code tweaking, I don't see a solution. However, if you use the Highlight library (highlight.el) then the solution is simple. Just use command hlt-highlight-symbol. It is bound by default to C-x X h s. (All of the commands inhighlight.el are prefixed with "hlt" and follow a consistent naming scheme.)

It's invalid for several reasons: cmp does not take 2 memory operands, very few instructions do and when they do, at most one of the memory operands is specified explicitly, the other is expected to be addressed in a predefined way implicitly without you telling it to the assembler. the size of the memory operand(s) is ambiguous, the assembler cannot figure out if it should be a byte, a word or a dword (double word). You should load one of the memory operands into a register and then do cmp with that register and with the other memory operand. Btw, the lines like mov [si],"$" may also have problem 2 described above, because a number (which "$" resolves into, the ASCII code of "$") doesn't have a size associated with it and the memory operand is bare, lacking the size specification. The correct/unambiguous way is to spell out the memory operand size like this: mov byte ptr [si],"$" Macros aren't subroutines, you don't call them: call Msg_display e1 This is invalid use of the macro. texts.full_text: and atheroma, and by monitoring the response to therapy. However, angioscopy requires a blood-free field for adequate visibility, and this may be difficult to obtain. The feasibility of carbon dioxide (CO2) and various saline delivery methods for clarifying the viewing field for percUtaneous angioscopy was investigated. Angioscopy of femoral and iliac arteries on nine dogs was performed using the CO2 and saline methods. The results showed that CO2 was more effective in providing a clear viewing field for angioscopy compared to the saline methods. The CO2 method also caused less trauma to the arterial walls and was associated with a faster recovery time for the animals. Furthermore, the CO2 method was found to be more cost-effective and less time-consuming compared to the saline methods. The use of CO2 as a clearing agent for the arterial walls also demonstrated a more consistent and reliable clearing effect, as the CO2 bubbles effectively dislodged the blood cells and debris, allowing for a more detailed and accurate angioscopic assessment. In conclusion, the use of CO2 as a blood-free angioscopic medium demonstrated significant advantages over the various saline delivery methods, particularly in terms of visibility, safety, and cost-effectiveness. This finding suggests that CO2 may be a more suitable and viable option for angioscopic procedures, particularly as the need for more precise and detailed vascular intervention continues to grow. Further research and clinical trials are warranted to fully explore the potential of CO2 as a standard angioscopic

The employer (the airline, ATS provider, Flightschool etc.) will be responsible for checking identity documents and for ensuring that candidates have a maximum of 45 minutes to complete the test. Individual pilots and controllers can also take the test independently in order to assess their own level. How is the test scored? The candidate receives points for each correct answer and zero points for each incorrect answer (there is no "negative marking"). For each question there is only one correct answer. Users receive a percentage score and the corresponding ICAO level score. How do I register? Complete the registration form here How do I pay? The fee is £20 GBP per test. Click the Pay Now button. After the payment is received then you will receive an email with your login details (within 2 working days) How quickly do I receive my results? Results will be emailed within 2 working days. b-TEA is an online, multiple-choice test which assesses a candidate's English ability for benchmarking / diagnostic purposes. The test assesses a candidate's knowledge of grammar, vocabulary and listening comprehension skills. It does not attempt to assess a candidate's spoken English.This means that it cannot be used for licensing purposes but it can be a simple, cheap and fast way of assessing a large number of people in order to determine their training needs. Mayflower College has tested a number of aviation specialists with both this Benchmark Test (b-TEA) and the full TEA test - the results of this research indicate an acceptable correlation between the two tests. Other times, their feedback might be along the lines of “Why don’t you do what I want you to do, or what I find valuable, or what I would do if it was my project, or what I’m used to seeing from other writers?” When I’m working on a creative project, I’m interested in developing it, not altering it to be something different. Just like a person—human beings are better off when you support them as they more fully become their own unique, amazing selves. Feedback at the beginning of a project In the beginning stages of a creative project, I’m very very selective about who I seek feedback from. A new original project is like a little sprout making its way up from the ground, powerful with potential, but vulnerable. It needs warmth and care and time to grow solid and strong. In the early stages, the only feedback that’s helpful to me is “What a cool idea; here are some possible resources for you.” At the beginning, a project is really between me and me. texts.full_text: like to be able to dynamically set which list to look up the items for based on a list ID, which is being passed in from props. Specifically, I'm using react-navigation to enter the ListPage, a view where a user can see the items on a List. So here's the code that gets executed when a user clicks on a list name and gets routed to the ListPage component: handleListSelection(list: Object) { const { navigation, userLists } = this.props; navigation.navigate('ListPage', { listID: list.record_id, listName: list.list_name, userLists, }); } From my previous (pre-AppSync/GraphQL) implementation, I know that I can access the list ID in ListPage via this.props.navigation.state.params.listID. I would like to be able to use that in my AppSync query, but because the query is created outside the component, I'm unable to access the props, and so am struggling to get the ID. A: Got this working using a package called react-apollo-dynamic-query which I found here. The author of that package also links directly to a simple function for doing what I'm trying to do here. Essentially it just wraps the regular graphql call in a simple way that exposes the props so they can be passed down to the query. texts.full_text: of Staphylococcus aureus small colony variant strains isolated fromItalian patients attending a regional cystic fibrosis care centre. Small colony variant (ScV) Staphylococcus aureus are a subpopulation of auxotroph, slow-growing strains causing persisting and relapsing infections in cysticfibrosis (CF) patients.Twenty-eight SCV and 29 normal S. aureus strains were isolated from 42 out of 222 Italian CF patients.The isol...were obtained froma regional cystic fibrosis care centre in Italy. The 28 SCV strains were identified in 42 out of 222 patients, while 29 normal S. aureus strains were isolated from 180 patients. The isolates were obtained from sputum, bronchial secretions, and respiratory cultures. The 28 SCVstrains were characterized by their slow growth rate, low biofilm formation, and reduced ability to produce virulence factors. In contrast, the 29 normalS. aureus strains were characterized by their rapid growth rate, highbiofilm formation, and ability to produce virulence factors. The SCV strains were found to be more susceptible to beta-lactam antibiotics and had reduced resistance to multiple antibiotics. The normal S. aureus strains, on the other hand, were more resistant to antibiotics. The characterization of SCV strains in CF patients is important for the developmentof effective treatment strategies and for the prevention of antibiotic resistance. texts.full_text: rominent reasons behind following such an approach to being: Anyone can Deploy The worst thing with a standard deployment structure, is that certain people do certain jobs. The infrastructure team will usually be tasked with deploying code across to all our environments. But what if they are away, or busy with other tasks? Should your deployments really be handled by real people? With Automated Builds and Deploys you remove this constraint from the process. You can deploy whenever you want, from wherever you are, and by whoever has the privileges to approve such a decision. Less Errors, More Repeat-ability It may take a little while to get it up and running, especially successfully, but once this hurdle has been overcome then Deploys will no longer contain human errors, and they can be run as many times as you want, whenever you want. You may have made a little text change somewhere, and want it pushed out, it’s a Saturday/Sunday. Continuous deployment doesn’t care, press a button and watch the magic happen! Developers and DevOps spend their time doing their job! Developers and Infrastructure will spend more time together getting this process in place. It’s integral the the current process is correctly defined and transformed into the automated process. This will require lots of trial and error, setting permissions, testing firewalls, creating re-runnable Powershell scripts and even ultimately automated sanity test scripts. This will free up time to look into the crux of the release, how to keep adding to it, optimising it, making it faster, more efficient, and ultimately give time back to the people that have more important thing to do then copy files from server to server! Release

texts.full_text: Edet Otobong is indeed an interesting figure within the world of football. Born on July 18, 1986, he hails from Douala and has been a stalwart in Cameroonian football representation across various leagues. Starting his career with Al-Ittihad, Edet's journey took him through several teams before settling into what appears to be an ongoing relationship with Misr El-Makasa. His versatility as well as the adaptability showcased by Otobong are evidenced in how he seamlessly moved from Sudanese football (Al-Hilal) and Moroccan leagues (Kawkab Marrakech), landing on Egyptian soil where his career took a significant leap with Misr El-Makasa. It's interesting to note that after gaining some experience there, Otobong further explored the possibilities of playing in Cyprus when he joined Kucuk kaymakli spor club. On an international scale, Edet has been involved at youth level for Cameroon by featuring in matches during All African games with their U-23 team. This indicates a talent recognized not just domestically but also internationally among footballing minds who value the development and potential of young athletes from across Africa on this competitive stage. His career is marked as much about journey and adaptation, which seems to be central themes for many international players - learning new cultures while bringing their own skills into foreign leagues. This kind of experience would not only make Edet a valuable player but also an important cultural ambassador wherever he plays next in his evolving footballing life story. While the article provides us with some basic information regarding Otobong's career, to gain further insights and comprehensive details about him such as personal achievements, milestones during matches or key moments of performance that have marked his journey would require looking at more specific sources like sports news articles from various seasons he was involved

texts.full_text: transition would require an unprecedented technological revolution, a complete overhaul of the world’s energy infrastructure, and a fundamental shift in lifestyles and economic priorities. The scale and complexity of this task are immense, and it is highly unlikely that it can be accomplished within the proposed timeframe solely through international agreements. The statement goes on to say that, "The main focus of climate policy should not be on reducing emissions but on adapting to climate change, as well as on developing strategies for enhancing carbon sequestration in forests, soils, wetlands, and oceans." This perspective challenges the prevailing narrative that mitigation is the primary solution to climate change, suggesting instead that adaptation and carbon sequestration are equally crucial. Albert Bates, the author of this piece, is an environmental lawyer, permaculture teacher, and co-founder of the Global Ecovillage Network. He argues that the current approach to addressing climate change is inadequate and that a more holistic and grounded strategy is necessary. Bates highlights the importance of local, community-based solutions and the development of resilient, self-sufficient ecosystems as we move towards the United Nations Climate Change Conference in Cancun (COP16). The article concludes by emphasizing the need for a paradigm shift, where we not only reduce our carbon footprint but also actively enhance natural carbon sinks

Check the `fig.yml` Configuration: Ensure that the Nginx service is defined in your `fig.yml` file and that it is correctly named.
